items.title: I Spent Two Years Building a Template That AI Replaced in Seconds
items.author: julien
items.content:
I spent two years building the “perfect” project template only to realize I had put myself in a chokehold. I was obsessed with code efficiency, architecture and last but not least, elegance. With AI completely disrupting our industry and setting new standards, I want to make a point why all of it still matters and why functionality over everything simply does not work, even in an AI-only development approach.

The two new camps in software development: pre-LLM vs. post-LLM

I went through the “old school” way of learning how to code: through university degrees of various kind which came with an annoying amount of problem sheets and and an even bigger pile of side-projects that rot somewhere on my disk. Education through universities is not dead (yet), but I strongly believe there is an “invisible” border between today’s juniors and seniors: pre- and post-LLM study cohorts.

We all know how tempting it is to simply throw every problem we’re confronted with into ChatGPT and use its answer, but you are missing out on the massive learnings your brain generates while trying to formulate the answer to your math problem or the final paragraph of your essay. The struggle is actually a feature, not a bug!

This is not a rant about the StackOverflow usage recession or how any generated code is AI slop or a bash of the new generation and how we did everything better back then. This is just to build common ground on how I used to learn things 10 years ago: software engineering classes, UML diagrams, user flow charts, class diagrams and endless problem sheets for seemingly useless or already solved problems. And sooner or later: Uncle Bob’s “Clean Code”.

From all the books I read for professional and/or educational purposes, this one stuck with me the most. Because it is very easy to follow. Simply everything makes sense, especially if you have not seen many codebases yet. What only appeared to me after some time in the industry: the learning curve the book sets out has a couple of local maxima and I’ve been trapped in one for quite some time.

The issue is: while I read the book, I built an idealistic view in my head. I started dreaming about the perfect codebase, the one where all developers could peace- and joyfully collaborate, the one nobody could complain about and that was resilient to any errors. But software is not ideal. Far from that. It is a compilation of calculated risk and trade-offs, hoping to optimize with regard to different metrics from one project to another.

As you progress in your life of a software engineer, side projects come sooner than you might think and appear everywhere you go. After I built some in my spare time, I immediately focused on inefficiencies in my (leisure time) development flow which in theory had to be way more efficient than my professional one, as I spent way less time on personal projects than on the ones at work.

So, with the clean code principles always in the back of my head, I did what had to be done: I built a starter project that consolidated all of my internal best practices, feature architecture, state management standards and applications of clean code principles that was used to “kickstart” every new private project I could think of.

Little did I know this would break my neck.

The Template Trap: How my productivity hit a wall of my own making.

“Why so negative?”, one could argue, so here goes: the fact that the template itself became the center of my efforts instead of the actual projects made me appreciate the journey less and I became over-focused on the result. I felt the need to squeeze out some kind of code out of any learning, so I wouldn’t make the same mistake again. So everything led to some abstraction of a feature, to make it reusable, to improve the state management or the error handling, or even the error messages displayed to the user.

Let me give you a specific example: integrating a simple API endpoint (with error/failure management) to a one page application all of a sudden turned into a new model, a new DTO, a new state management instance for a clear separation of concerns, an extension of the error message service, a new method in the domain and data layer and then an implementation of the data layer (just in case I felt like changing the database structure of the side project I won’t ever touch again anyways).

I basically built a harness that I wanted to apply to every single one of my project. But the issue with harnesses is: they may provide stability, but they can become too tight. And balancing the “template harness” to hit just the right amount of guard railing and helping you start faster, but not blocking your new features and having to rebuild everything is a tough one, especially if you try to wing it in your spare time. The irony was: I started spending more time improving the template than actually building features. I fell right for the classical trap of over-abstraction, leading to me having to “tweak the code a little” for almost every new project. What a great way to waste time but still feel productive!

To simply break down everything I wrote down until now: I built a static solution for a highly dynamic problem.

I wanted a waterproof 99% template where you only need to fill the gaps and add custom features where needed. This approach is as static as it gets. But software engineering does not work that way. Especially not such a fast-paced environment such as web development. Decisions I make today may be suboptimal in 3-6 months time or even bad within a year. Ironically, it is the “laziness” of the programmer that led to me having to do way more work. I basically built myself a harness that was a bit too tight and had to rip it open and resow it for every project.

Was Uncle Bob wrong? No. I just didn’t get what he meant to say. Uncle Bob taught us how to write clean code, but he couldn't teach us when to stop. My mistake wasn't following his principles, it was trying to automate them before I understood the context.

The Death of the Boilerplate

Then, rescue came: vibe coding tools. To be perfectly clear: I see AI and all agentic/vibe coding tools as expert tools and strongly believe they should be used as such, as otherwise, the well-known phenomenon of “AI slop” will forcingly occur. After getting up to speed with the various tools that were released, I quickly realized: the rigid harnesses I just described could be maintained, but became much easier to handle. My static templates all of a sudden became generative standards that I could write down in form of system prompts, rules, markdown files, cursor rules or whatever else floated the tool’s boat! Finally, a way to make sure my learnings, standards and best practices were respected, but took away the pain from the tearing and custom resowing of the harness!

The software engineer of the future is not represented by the currently growing population of (sloppy) vibe coders, nor will the manual architects survive the current transition. The future lies in the ones that learnt what good, maintainable, reliable code looks like and that are able to prompt the slop out of the AI. Seasoned developers need to learn how to inject their knowledge into AI systems and how to leverage their experience and expertise, juniors and students need to keep learning to build skills and resist the trap of outsourcing everything to AI.

Some compare the AI disruption in the software engineering industry to the steam engine in the 19th century. The same principle applies: in the same way skilled workers survived the introduction of the steam engine, the skilled computer scientists will survive the full adoption of AI-driven development.

I recently heard Andy Yen (Proton’s CEO) say “AI will replace programmers, but we’ll still need judgement and a sense of taste [for good code]”. Those are two skills that take a long time to build and that have no shortcuts. If I am to give any advice then it would be this: build skill, not tool competence.

items.title: Agent Harnessing: The Non-Model Infrastructure That Makes AI Agents Actually Work
items.author: Tosin Kolawole
items.content:
The model is not the hard part.

Every frontier model has a story about their latest capabilities. Claude Opus 4.7, GPT-5.5, and Gemini 3.1 Pro are genuinely remarkable reasoning engines; each pushing further on context, multimodal perception, and agentic tool use. Behind each of these extraordinary reasoning engines, however, is a non-model infrastructure layer working silently to make the model useful in practice.

Everyone building AI agents have access to the same frontier models through the same APIs. Often times, what separates a production-grade agent from one that fails silently on the third real workload is not the model. It is the infrastructure surrounding it.

That infrastructure is the .

Agent harnessing is the non-model layer that gives an agent persistent memory, tool connectivity, controlled reasoning loops, verification, and the ability to coordinate with other agents. The model decides what to do. The harness determines whether it actually does it, reliably, at scale, across the full range of things that go wrong.

This article covers what that infrastructure contains and a brief introduction on how to implement it.

\ A popular way to think about agent architecture has been:

Agent = LLM + Memory + Planning + Tool Use

That still works for simple deployments. But a more complete picture comes from Agentic Artificial Intelligence (Ehtesham et al., 2026), which proposes a unified taxonomy breaking agents into six dimensions:

Agent = Perception + Brain + Planning + Action + Tool Use + Collaboration

I find this a significantly better starting point than the older formula. Each dimension has its own failure modes, its own design decisions, and its own place in the harness. The older formula bundles too much together and hides exactly the problems that bite hardest in production.

That said, the taxonomy I use throughout this article is a personal adaptation of the original. I elevate Memory as a distinct, standalone dimension rather than folding it into the Brain, which is where the paper positions it. That choice reflects a practical conviction I have built from working with production systems; memory is its own engineering discipline with its own tooling, its own failure modes, and an implementation surface that is genuinely independent from the reasoning engine. Collapsing it into Brain understates how much work it actually demands. My adapted framing becomes:

Agent = Perception + Brain + Memory + Planning + Action + Collaboration

is how the agent receives and preprocesses its inputs. Claude Opus 4.6 and GPT-5.4 both handle text, images, audio, and structured data natively, so this is no longer just a text problem. What gets passed into the context window, in what form, and at what level of compression is a harness decision, not a model decision.

is the reasoning engine. Agents today are not single-model systems. Claude Haiku 4.5 handles fast, low-complexity work like extraction and classification. Claude Sonnet 4.6 handles the bulk of reasoning and coding. Claude Opus 4.6 or GPT-5.4 steps in for deep orchestration and high-stakes decisions. The harness decides which model handles which task.

has grown into its own engineering discipline. More on this in the next section.

now comes in two flavours. The ReAct loop reasons and acts at every step, which is flexible but token-heavy. Plan-and-execute separates the thinking from the doing, breaking the task into a dependency graph upfront and executing steps in parallel where possible. The harness decides which pattern fits the task.

has moved on from individual JSON function calls. The dominant pattern today is code-as-action: the agent writes a script that calls multiple tools, handles retries in code, processes the results, and returns a single clean output. This keeps the context window lean and cuts costs significantly on tool-heavy pipelines.

is now handled at the protocol level through MCP and A2A rather than baked into individual frameworks. More on this in the multi-agent section.

The reason I find this six-part framing more useful than the older four-part formula is straightforward: it forces one to reason about failure at the right granularity. When an agent goes wrong in production, knowing which of the six dimensions failed tells exactly where in the harness to look. The older formula collapses that precision into ambiguity, and ambiguity is expensive when something breaks at two in the morning.

1.2 What the Harness Is Actually Made Of

Agent harness has six distinct components. Each has its own failure modes and its own implementation surface.

is what controls what the model reasons over. Most agent failures I have seen are not only the model giving a wrong answer; they are the model reasoning over the wrong information because the harness injected too much, too little, or the wrong kind of context. The harness is responsible for selecting what to retrieve, compressing what exceeds the context window, and filtering out noise before it reaches the model.

gives the agent continuity across steps and sessions. Without it, every session starts from scratch.

give the agent the ability to act in the world rather than just produce text.

governs the reasoning loop: when to continue, when to stop, and what to do when something goes wrong.

independently checks whether the agent’s output actually meets the required standard before the task is closed.

manages how multiple agents communicate and divide work.

None of these are model capabilities. All of them are harness responsibilities.

2.1 Implementing Context Management: The Harness Starts Before Reasoning Begins

Context management is upstream of everything. What enters the context window determines what the model can reason about.

The four operations harness should implement are:

retrieve only what is relevant to the current step. In a RAG pipeline, this means running a semantic search over knowledge base and injecting the top-ranked chunks, not the entire document store. For instance, a legal review agent searching a 500-document repository should receive the 6 most relevant contract clauses, not all 500 documents.

when context is long, summarise it rather than truncate it blindly. Anthropic’s Agent SDK provides automatic compaction, but for tasks spanning multiple sessions, the harness should also write a structured progress file at the end of each session. This file records what was completed, what is pending, and what the next session should prioritise. Compaction alone does not preserve this kind of structured state.

Inputs should be filtered at the tool boundary before they reach the model. A database query returning 10,000 rows should be aggregated to a summary before injection. This is both a cost control and a prompt injection defence. When an agent reads web pages or user-submitted documents, adversarial instructions in that content can redirect the agent’s behaviour unless the harness sanitises inputs first.

externalise information the agent will need later. Scratchpads, progress files, and structured state objects should be written by the harness at each checkpoint, not left to the model to reconstruct from memory.

Memory should be implemented as a layered system, not a single database. Each layer handles a different scope.

is the active context window. It is managed by the context engineering operations above.

uses a vector database to store facts extracted from past interactions and retrieve them when relevant.

For example, Mem0 is an open-source library that acts as a dedicated memory layer sitting between an agent and its underlying language model. Mem0 converts conversations into atomic memory facts, stores them in a vector store (Qdrant, pgvector, or Chroma), and retrieves them via similarity search at the start of each reasoning cycle.

The implementation involves three steps. First, instantiate a object, which by default uses an in-memory store suitable for development. Second, call memory.add(messages, user_id=user_id) after each interaction; Mem0 extracts the facts and writes them to the configured store. Third, call memory.search(query=message, filters={"user_id": user_id}, top_k=5) at the start of each new session to retrieve the most relevant stored facts. The retrieved facts are then injected into the system prompt before the model call.

\ Persistent cross-session memory: Involves storing information as files in a managed directory, reading and writing those files across entirely separate conversations. The agent reads, writes, and updates these files through tool calls. This is the right layer for tasks that run over multiple days, where the agent needs to maintain a coherent project model across sessions.

2.3 Implementing the Control Loop

The agent control loop runs five stages in sequence: Perceive, Reason, Plan, Act, Observe. The model handles the reasoning. The harness governs the loop itself.

Perceive → Reason → Plan → Act → Observe → [repeat]

The most important harness decision here is termination. A loop without stopping conditions runs until it exhausts your budget. Layer four stopping conditions:

  • involves setting an explicit turn cap matched to the task. A conversational agent handling a single user request typically finishes in one to three turns. A coding agent working through a complex multi-file refactor may need twenty or more. The right cap is specific to task, not a universal number. Set low enough to catch runaway loops and high enough not to interrupt legitimate work.
  • involves setting a per-run token limit. Single-agent loops consume roughly four times the tokens of equivalent chat interactions. Multi-agent systems push that to fifteen times or more.
  • if the last two or three iterations produced no new information or no change in state, exit loop and escalate.
  • involves verifying at each iteration whether the task objective has actually been met, not just whether the agent believes it has.

The core value of MCP involves: implement a tool once as an MCP server and every compatible agent can call it without additional integration work. What matters more for harness design is how MCP changes the way tool interactions are structured inside the loop.

The most useful pattern for tool-heavy agents is what Anthropic calls code-as-action. Rather than routing individual MCP tool calls sequentially through the agent loop which floods the context window with intermediate results; the agent writes a short script that calls multiple tools, joins the results, handles retries in code, and returns a single clean output. For instance, an agent that needs to query three APIs, filter for anomalies, and return a summary should write a Python script to do all of that and execute it once. The model sees the final output, not the raw result of each intermediate step. This reduces context bloat significantly on anything involving more than two tool calls in sequence.

Prompt injection defence at the tool boundary should also be implemented. Every piece of content entering through a tool call; web pages, user-uploaded documents, external API responses — is untrusted input that could contain adversarial instructions. The harness sanitises that content before it reaches the model, using boundary markers and pattern stripping at the tool output layer.

2.5 Implementing Multi-Agent Coordination

In practice, multi-agent systems add real coordination overhead and that overhead needs to be justified by something a single-agent approach genuinely cannot handle. For instance, parallel execution across subtasks, workloads that exceed a single context window, or tasks that benefit from meaningfully different model capabilities at different stages. If none of those conditions apply, multi-agent coordination is not really worth it.

When multi-agent architecture is the right call, the harness becomes responsible for something a single-agent system never has to handle: the structural relationship between agents. This includes how agents discover each other, how work is delegated and returned, how results from multiple agents are synthesised, and what happens when one agent in the chain fails or times out. None of this is handled by the models themselves. All of it lives in the harness.

In other words, the harness wires this together across three distinct layers.

sits in the harness, not in any individual agent. The orchestrator agent receives a task, decomposes it into subtasks based on criteria defined in the harness, and delegates each subtask to the appropriate specialist. That routing logic; which agent handles which subtask, under what conditions, with what fallback; is a harness decision.

are governed by the A2A protocol. The harness registers each agent’s Agent Card at startup, defining its capabilities, accepted inputs, and expected outputs. When the orchestrator delegates, it queries the registry, selects the right specialist, and hands off a structured task payload via the A2A client. The specialist processes the task, returns a structured result, and the harness feeds that result back into the orchestrator’s reasoning cycle. Crucially, the orchestrator does not need to know which tools the specialist used or how it was implemented. The harness enforces that separation.

is a harness responsibility that is easy to overlook. When two or three specialist agents return results in parallel, something has to combine them coherently before the final response is produced. That synthesis step; whether it is a second model call, a deterministic merge function, or a structured aggregation, is defined in the harness; not improvised by the model at runtime.

Human-in-the-loop gates are the final coordination responsibility that should be encoded in the harness. The model should not decide when to involve a human. The harness should define this explicitly: which action classes require approval before execution, what the escalation path is when an agent cannot resolve an ambiguity, and what happens if approval does not arrive within a defined timeout.

Often times, models report task completion based on their own assessment of their output. That assessment is unreliable. Recently, Anthropic’s coding agent research found that agents would consistently mark features as complete without verifying they worked in the actual running application. The agent had written the code, run some unit tests, and declared success. The feature was broken in ways that only became visible when a real user tried to use it.

The solution was not to instruct the agent to be more careful. It was to add browser automation through Puppeteer, forcing the agent to verify features the way a user would: opening the application, clicking through the relevant flow, and checking the actual state change. The harness added an independent verification step that the agent could not short-circuit.

This is the evaluator-optimiser pattern: one agent or call generates an output, a separate evaluation step checks it against defined criteria, and structured feedback is fed back into the generation step if the criteria are not met. The loop continues until the output passes or a maximum iteration count is reached.

The evaluation step can be:

  • A separate model call with an explicit rubric and scoring instructions
  • An automated test suite that runs against the output programmatically
  • A structured evaluation agent with access to domain-specific validation tools
  • Browser or UI automation that verifies the output in an actual running environment

The model is the reasoning engine. The model gets the benchmarks, the harness earns the reliability.

Memory without structure loses state. Context without selection generates noise. Tool calls without verification produce confident wrong answers. Loops without stopping conditions exhaust budgets. Multi-agent systems without protocols create brittle custom bridges.

Agent harnessing is that infrastructure. It is the memory system that ensures an agent picks up where it left off instead of starting over. It is the context layer that ensures the model is reasoning over the right information rather than noise. It is the verification loop that catches errors before they propagate. It is the stopping conditions that prevent runaway loops. It is the routing logic that balances capability against cost. It is the protocol layer that makes agents composable across vendor boundaries. It is the observability stack that makes failures diagnosable.

Everyone building have access to the same frontier models through the same APIs. The differentiator is harness maturity: how systematically and deliberately the non-model infrastructure has been designed, instrumented, and improved.

Build the harness. Everything else follows from it.

Ehtesham, A., Hassan, A., Abu-Salih, S. and Abuhmed, T. (2026) Agentic Artificial Intelligence: Architectures, Taxonomies, and Evaluation of Large Language Model Agents, arXiv:2601.12560. Available at: https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.12560

Research and engineering references: Anthropic engineering blog, OpenAI system cards and field reports, Linux Foundation Agentic AI Foundation documentation, arXiv 2601.12560 (January 2026), Mem0 ECAI 2025 paper, LangChain context engineering documentation (July 2025).

items.title: The AI Compute Crunch Is Here (and It's Affecting the Entire Economy)
items.author: Jason Koebler
items.content:
Earlier this week, I wrote an article about startups that are spending money on AI compute (tokens on tools like Claude and OpenAI’s products) rather than hiring human employees. There are all sorts of ways this business strategy could fail, and we are beginning to see signs that one of the most obvious ones could be coming to pass: AI companies can’t endlessly subsidize their AI products by charging users less than it costs to actually run them.

This is the AI compute crunch, and the signs are all around us: 

  • it is pausing new signups for Copilot, tightening usage limits, and removing access to several more expensive AI models. 
  • Anthropic has tightened access to Claude Code, and tested removing access to Claude Code entirely in its $20 per month plan (keeping access in its $100 per month plan)
  • , Anthropic restricted Claude access to users of OpenClaw because the heavy usage was unsustainable
  • OpenAI’s CFO Sarah Friar has been talking endlessly about how the company does not have enough compute, which has manifested in decisions like deciding to shut down Sora
  • Software that has AI tools embedded in them have increased between 20 and 37 percent according to some analysts; this has included increases in prices for Microsoft 365, Notion’s Business plan, Salesforce, and Google Workspace prices

But it’s not just that AI companies are restricting access to their products, shutting down products altogether, and beginning to increase prices. The broader impact of the current unsustainability of AI can be seen across various sectors of the economy. 

  • RAM, graphics cards, and hard drive / solid state storage for consumers have skyrocketed in price and are sold out in many stores. The same 2TB external SSD I bought late last year cost me $159 at the time, cost $449 a month ago, and .
  • Similarly, the general cost of consumer electronics is increasing as chip manufacturers and production lines shift their focus to building more AI capacity. The largest consumer electronics manufacturer in the world, Apple, says it is having trouble securing chipmaking capacity for upcoming iPhones. 
  • Home electric bill costs have skyrocketed in some states with high concentrations of AI data centers, leading in part to a widespread, concerted effort by some towns and states to reject and restrict new data centers entirely. There is a fear among experts that similar shortages and price increases could come for water supplies as well.

What this means is that the age of cheap, underpriced AI appears to be ending, or at least the compute crunch means the venture capitalists and investment firms funding OpenAI and Anthropic are going to have to be willing to burn even more cash in order to continue subsidizing their products. 

, I compared this situation to Uber (and any number of fast-scaling startups that sought to lock in customers then jack up prices). This comparison is only useful in that, like Uber, what AI companies are doing to this point is wildly unsustainable and is being subsidized by investors. For years, Uber’s investors subsidized the cost of individual Uber rides to keep prices for consumers artificially low in order to gain market share, crush competition, and destroy the taxi industry. Uber and its investors could only lose money on each ride for so long as it continued to burn cash. This eventually led to enshittification for both riders and drivers as Uber suddenly jacked up prices for consumers and sought to find ways to pay drivers less. The difference, as Ed Zitron has pointed out, is that Uber’s costs were extremely low because Uber is essentially an app that owns none of the infrastructure, and so jacking up the cost of its service went quite a bit further toward getting it to break even. 

Some version of this is coming for AI companies, but the path toward sustainability is far more complicated because of the enormous infrastructure and societal costs of scaling AI even further. “Make Claude more expensive and limit its services” is a lever Anthropic can pull, but AI companies are also burning money trying to build new data centers, juggling the political backlash to those data centers, fending off various copyright and public safety lawsuits, and spending huge amounts of money trying to train the next frontier versions of their large language models. None of this is remotely sustainable as it currently stands. 

This means that the startups that are using AI agents to scale their operations are doing so at a time when AI costs are unsustainably low and may wake up one day to find that their compute costs suddenly double, 10x, or that they simply aren’t able to access compute anymore. 

The general, long-term hope for the AI industry seems to be one in which multiple things need to happen to avoid a broader AI bubble burst. There needs to be a widespread renewable energy revolution (which society and our environment desperately needs), vastly increased chip and component manufacturing, and models need to become more efficient. On top of that, AI needs to be widely adopted and prove to be enduringly useful and reliable across a bunch of different sectors and use cases, something the jury is still very much out on (and some studies have already shown AI use is creating more work for humans, not less). All of this must happen while AI continues to put pressures on these systems that are making the problem worse (AI is making energy more expensive in the short term; lots of data centers are powered by fossil fuels; AI is pushing up the costs of components, chips, and gadgets, etc). 

Finally, all of this must happen while society juggles whatever potential mass unemployment / economic fallout comes from AI and the ensuing problems this causes for these employee-less companies who expect to sell their products to a populous that is struggling to find work. As many commenters pointed out in response to my last story: If companies begin replacing their employees with AI agents, who are they going to sell their products to?

items.title: Foundation Blueprints: 5 Production-Ready Patterns for Backend Systems
items.author: Puneet Chandel
items.content:
Every engineering team eventually hits the same realization: the systems they build, regardless of domain, fall into a surprisingly small number of architectural shapes.

After building production systems across multiple domains, a clear pattern emerges. Most systems map to one of . Every new project starts with one question: 

These are AWS-native, production-ready blueprints, each hardened with cross-cutting concerns for security, reliability, and observability.

The 5 Foundation Blueprints

| # | Blueprint | When to reach for it | |----|----|----| | 1 | | Move data in bulk, on a schedule, or through a transformation pipeline | | 2 | Event-Driven Orchestration | React to upstream changes and coordinate downstream actions | | 3 | | Serve synchronous request/response traffic | | 4 | | An LLM is a first-class participant in a business workflow | | 5 | | Answer "what happened" at scale: read-heavy, historical, aggregated |

Pick any system in your organization. It maps to one of these, or it's a composition of two.

| Blueprint | Language | Framework / Libraries | Compute | |----|----|----|----| | Data Movement | Python | boto3, pandas, requests, AWS Glue, Step Functions | ECS Fargate (scheduled) + Glue | | Event-Driven Orchestration | Kotlin/Java | Spring Boot, Hexagonal architecture, Kafka clients, SQS listeners | ECS Fargate (always-on) | | Real-Time APIs | Kotlin/Java | Spring Boot, Spring Security, Hexagonal architecture | ECS Fargate (always-on, auto-scaling) | | AI / Agent Workflows | Python | LangGraph, LangChain, FastMCP, boto3 | ECS Fargate | | Reporting & Analytics | SQL + Python | Athena, Glue, QuickSight | Serverless |

 The tech stacks listed here are reference implementations, not prescriptions. The blueprints are design patterns that work with similar technologies. Spring Boot could be Micronaut or Quarkus. ECS Fargate could be Kubernetes. Kafka could be Kinesis.

Blueprint I: Data Movement

Exports, sync, scheduled jobs, file processing, ETL

Anything that moves data from point A to point B in bulk or on a schedule.

 EventBridge triggers ECS Fargate Tasks that pull data from source systems, transform it, and write to S3 or push downstream. Ephemeral compute, no idle cost.

 Step Functions orchestrates AWS Glue jobs (Extract → Transform → Load) for heavier transformation pipelines. Declarative configuration over custom code.

The VPC hosts both compute paths in a private subnet. ECS Fargate Tasks handle scheduled exports and syncs. AWS Glue handles ETL pipelines, with Step Functions orchestrating the job sequence. Both paths access external systems through a NAT gateway and AWS services through VPC endpoints.

Key services: EventBridge (triggers), S3 (storage), Step Functions (orchestration), AWS Glue (ETL), Data Catalog (schema registry), Secrets Manager, CloudWatch Logs, ECR.

  • Nightly or hourly data exports
  • Incremental syncs between platforms
  • File ingestion and transformation (CSV, XML, JSON)
  • Batch cleanup or reconciliation jobs

Blueprint II : Event-Driven Orchestration & Integration

Domain events, async workflows, webhook delivery, real-time streaming

Something happens upstream (an order is placed, a payment is received, a state changes) and the system coordinates a reaction across multiple services.

Workers follow a simple loop: consume → process → publish. Each ECS Service runs independently, polling its message source, processing events, and publishing results or triggering downstream actions. Multiple worker services can run in parallel, each handling different event types.

DynamoDB stores idempotency keys, workflow state, and checkpoints. Every handler safely reprocesses duplicate events. At-least-once delivery is a feature, not a problem.

Multiple ECS Clusters run in private subnets, each hosting independent worker services. Workers consume from message brokers (SNS, SQS, Apache Kafka) and write state to DynamoDB. External systems connect through the NAT gateway.

Key services: SNS (fan-out), SQS (queuing + DLQ), Apache Kafka (streaming), DynamoDB (state + idempotency), S3 (event payloads), VPC endpoints.

  •  DynamoDB conditional writes ensure duplicate messages are no-ops.
  •  Queue depth absorbs bursts; workers scale on queue depth.
  •  Separate queues per consumer; one failure doesn't block others.
  •  Events re-driven from DLQ after remediation. No data lost.
  • Order placed → trigger fulfillment, inventory, and notification workflows
  • Payment received → update records, send receipt
  • Webhook delivery to external partners with retries
  • CDC streams from databases to downstream consumers
  • Any workflow spanning multiple services with partial failure handling

Blueprint 3: Real-Time APIs

Domain APIs, synchronous request/response, gateway

A client (web app, mobile app, partner integration, automation platform) asks a question and expects an immediate answer.

The request path: Client → Route 53 → ALB (public subnet) → ECS Service (private subnet). Cognito handles OAuth2 authentication, Certificate Manager provides TLS, and WAF filters malicious traffic. Spring Security enforces scope-based authorization via JWT.

APIs aren't isolated. They can publish events to SQS or Kafka for async side-effects, connecting directly to Blueprint 2 for downstream processing.

ALB sits in the public subnet, routing to ECS Services in the private subnet. The service layer can access relational data (RDS), key-value data (DynamoDB), object storage (S3), and push events to message brokers (SQS, Kafka). External system calls go through the NAT gateway.

Key services: Route 53 (DNS), ALB (load balancing + TLS 1.3), Cognito (auth), Certificate Manager, WAF, RDS, DynamoDB, S3, SQS, Kafka, CloudWatch Logs.

| Component | Configuration | |----|----| | ALB | TLS 1.3, health checks, HTTP→HTTPS redirect | | ECS | 2-10 tasks, 1 vCPU, 2GB memory, auto-scaling on CPU/request count | | Cognito | OAuth2 client credentials, JWT validation | | DynamoDB | On-demand capacity, GSI indexes, TTL enabled |

  • Internal teams need programmatic access to domain data
  • Mobile or web apps need synchronous CRUD operations
  • Partner integrations requiring request/response APIs
  • Automation platforms orchestrating across services
  • Any use case where the caller expects an immediate response

Blueprint 4: AI / Agent Workflows

LLM inference, RAG pipelines, multi-step agents, human-in-the-loop

When an LLM is a first-class participant in a business workflow. Not a chatbot, but an autonomous agent that reasons, uses tools, and takes action.

External events or webhooks arrive via API Gateway → SQS. An ECS Fargate worker picks up the message and invokes the LangGraph orchestration graph, managing multi-step agent execution, tool calls, branching, and retries.

Two ECS services work together:  handles orchestration and LLM calls to AWS Bedrock, while FastMCP provides standardized tool integration with external systems via the Model Context Protocol.

Amazon Bedrock provides three capabilities:  (Claude Sonnet 4),  for RAG (vector search over your documents), and  for versioned, auditable prompt templates.

A  from end users flows back into DynamoDB, enabling continuous improvement of agent behavior. This closed-loop learning system is what makes AI workflows fundamentally different from traditional event-driven systems.

ECS Cluster in a private subnet runs two services: the LangGraph orchestrator and the FastMCP tool server. VPC endpoints connect to Amazon Bedrock (LLM, Knowledge Base, Prompt Management). External systems are reached through the NAT gateway.

Key services: API Gateway (event ingestion), SQS (queuing + DLQ), Amazon Bedrock (LLM + RAG + Prompt Management), DynamoDB (workflow state, feedback), S3 (agent outputs), Kafka, CloudWatch Logs.

| Layer | Choice | |----|----| | Orchestration | LangGraph | | LLM provider | AWS Bedrock (Claude Sonnet 4) | | RAG | AWS Bedrock Knowledge Base | | Prompt management | AWS Bedrock Prompt Management | | Tool integration | FastMCP (MCP servers for external systems) | | State management | LangGraph DynamoDBSaver | | Event ingestion | API Gateway → SQS |

What makes AI workflows different

Both Blueprint 2 and Blueprint 4 are event-driven. Both use SQS and ECS. But AI workflows have unique requirements:

  • Non-deterministic execution. Same input, different outputs. Needs prompt versioning and output logging.
  • Stateful multi-step agents. LangGraph manages tool-calling loops, branching, and human-in-the-loop checkpoints.
  •  Vector search against a knowledge base adds a retrieval layer.
  •  Explicit user feedback enables continuous improvement. Traditional workflows don't have this.
  • Automated document analysis and summarization
  • Intelligent routing with context-aware reasoning
  • Root cause analysis triggered by system events
  • Knowledge-base-powered Q&A
  • Any workflow where LLM reasoning replaces manual human judgment

Blueprint 5: Reporting & Analytics

Dashboards, aggregations, historical queries, analytics

This blueprint answers "what happened?" It's fundamentally different from APIs: read-heavy, historical, and optimized for query flexibility over write speed. It naturally comes last because . Data Movement exports land in S3, and Reporting queries them.

Data flows through three stages:

  1.  (Data Movement) pulls data from external systems (FTP, streams, databases, APIs) and lands it in the  (S3).
  2.  orchestrate  that transform raw data into  layers: cleaning, partitioning, denormalizing, and pre-computing rollups.
  3.  registers schemas and partitions.  queries the processed/aggregated data via serverless SQL.  serves dashboards and embedded analytics.

This blueprint is entirely serverless. Blueprint 1 handles the ingestion. Glue handles the transformation. Athena handles the queries. QuickSight handles the visualization.

The data lake is organized in S3:  (ingested as-is),  (cleaned, partitioned, Parquet),  (pre-computed rollups). Data Catalog sits alongside Glue, serving as the shared schema registry that Athena and QuickSight both reference.

Key services: S3 (data lake), AWS Glue (ETL + Data Catalog), Step Functions (orchestration), Amazon Athena (serverless SQL), Amazon QuickSight (dashboards + embedded analytics).

  • S3 + Athena over Redshift. Serverless querying over a well-partitioned data lake is cheaper and simpler for most workloads. Reach for Redshift only when you need sub-second latency at massive scale.
  •  Raw is the safety net (always reprocess from source), processed is the workhorse, aggregated is the speed layer.
  • Glue Data Catalog as shared schema. Glue jobs, Athena, and QuickSight all reference the same catalog. One source of truth for table definitions.
  • Partition by date and domain. Keeps Athena scans targeted and costs predictable.
  • Executive dashboards (revenue trends, pipeline velocity)
  • Tenant-level usage analytics
  • Compliance and audit reporting
  • Historical trend analysis
  • Ad-hoc data exploration by analysts

Foundation Blueprints define  you build. Cross-cutting concerns define . These are baked into every blueprint from day one.

Reliability & failure handling

| Concern | What it does | Tech stack | |----|----|----| | Circuit breaker / retry / backoff | Fails fast when downstream services are unhealthy. Retries with exponential backoff and jitter. | Resilience4j (JVM), tenacity (Python) | | Idempotency | Duplicate messages are safe no-ops. | DynamoDB conditional writes | | Dead letter queue + replay | Failed messages preserved. Re-driven after remediation. | SQS DLQ, CloudWatch Alarms | | Graceful shutdown | Finishes in-flight work before exiting on SIGTERM. | Spring Boot shutdown hooks (JVM), signal handlers (Python) |

Concurrency & consistency

| Concern | What it does | Tech stack | |----|----|----| | Concurrency control | Optimistic locking. Racing writes fail and retry, not silently overwrite. | DynamoDB @DynamoDbVersionAttribute (JVM), conditionExpression (Python) | | Rate limiting / throttling | Respects SaaS API limits outbound. Enforces per-client throttling inbound. | Resilience4j RateLimiter (JVM), ratelimit (Python), API Gateway throttling | | Back-pressure | Queue depth absorbs spikes. Workers scale on queue depth. | SQS + ECS auto-scaling on ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible | | Distributed locking | Exactly-once across multiple tasks. TTL-based leases. Used sparingly. | DynamoDB conditional writes with TTL |

| Concern | What it does | Tech stack | |----|----|----| | Structured logging | JSON logs with correlation ID, service name, environment. | SLF4J + Logback (JVM), structlog (Python), DataDog Log Management | | Distributed tracing | Single correlation ID across the full request chain. | Micrometer + DataDog APM (JVM), ddtrace (Python) | | Metrics / alerting (RED) | Rate, Errors, Duration per service. Anomaly-based alerts. | Micrometer (JVM), DataDog StatsD (Python), CloudWatch Alarms | | Health checks | /health (liveness) and /ready (readiness). ALB routes only to healthy tasks. | Spring Boot Actuator (JVM), custom endpoints (Python) |

| Concern | What it does | Tech stack | |----|----|----| | Zero-trust / auth | Services authenticate to each other. No implicit network trust. | Cognito (OAuth2), Spring Security (JVM), WAF | | Secrets management | No secrets in code or images. Rotated automatically. | AWS Secrets Manager | | Scope-based authorization | JWT tokens carry scopes. Read-only services can't write. | Spring Security (JVM), custom JWT middleware (Python) |

| Concern | What it does | Tech stack | |----|----|----| | Feature flags | Ship behind flags. Roll out gradually. Kill the flag if broken. | LaunchDarkly | | Strangler fig migration | Incrementally route traffic from legacy to new. No big-bang rewrites. | API Gateway weighted routing, ALB path-based routing | | Config externalization | Same image runs in QA, staging, production. | AWS Secrets Manager, AWS Parameter Store | | Infrastructure as code | All infrastructure versioned and reviewed like application code. | Terraform |

\ A note on what's deliberately left out. These blueprints are infrastructure patterns. They define how systems are built and deployed. How you model the domain  each blueprint (bounded contexts, aggregate design, domain events, ubiquitous language) is a separate discipline that deserves its own treatment. DDD shapes what goes inside the blueprint. The blueprint shapes how it runs.

That's the power of Foundation Blueprints. Not more patterns to learn. Fewer decisions to make.

The input subsystem saw several drivers removed this week for decades old hardware...</article>

items.title: Warner Bros CEO David Zaslav’s $550 Million Golden Parachute Sees ‘Symbolic’ Investor Rebuke
items.author: Karl Bode
items.content:
Zaslav oversaw years of dysfunction during the last wave of pointless Warner Brothers mergers, which included tens of thousands of brutal layoffs, consistent creative infighting, endlessly higher prices, cancelled programming, and a steady wave of overall dysfunction. And that’s before we even get to this latest merger with Paramount, which is expected to see more layoffs and chaos than ever.

It’s nice that investors took the time to realize these outsized compensation packages not only harm the company’s bottom line, but they reward incompetent leadership. But at the same time they approved a merger that, largely thanks to the whopping $111 billion in debt, is inevitably going to result in yet more layoffs, price hikes, customer defections, and overall chaos.

Investors turn a blind eye because they like the temporary stock bumps and tax breaks generated from pointless consolidation, but you’d think that the fact that every single Warner Brothers mega-transaction to date has been a giant disaster would be more foreword in their thinking.

It’s the extraction class abusing the rules of the game to pretend to be good at business. They’re not actually building anything useful, nor are they remotely interested in the longevity of the company, its customers, the talent that powers it, or the people who work there. They’re playing with funny numbers to try and perpetually generate the illusion of impossible permanent growth at incredible scale, then cashing out when the check finally comes due for their complicated shell games.

items.title: In another wild turn for AI chips, Meta signs deal for millions of Amazon AI CPUs
items.author: Julie Bort
items.content: <article>Meta has commandeered a big chunk of Amazon's homegrown CPUs (not GPUs) for AI agentic workloads, signaling that a new kind of chip race has begun.</article>

items.title: Claude Is Connecting Directly To Your Personal Apps
items.author: BeauHD
items.content:
Anthropic is expanding Claude's app integrations beyond work tools, adding personal-service connectors like Spotify, Uber, AllTrails, TripAdvisor, Instacart, and TurboTax. The Verge reports: Some of these apps, such as Spotify, already have similar connectors in OpenAI's ChatGPT. Once an app is connected, Claude will suggest relevant connected apps directly in your conversations, like using AllTrails for hike recommendations. Anthropic notes in its blog post announcing the new connectors that, "Your data from [connected apps] isn't used to train our models, and the app doesn't see your other conversations with Claude. You can also disconnect it at any time."
 
Additionally, Anthropic says "there are no paid placements or sponsored answers in conversations with Claude." When multiple apps seem relevant, Claude will show results from both "ranked by what's most useful." Claude will also ask users to verify before taking actions like making a purchase or reservation using a connected app.

items.title: Meet the Writer: Hacker Noon's Contributor Meisa Chen on Exploring Game Science
items.author: meichenster
items.content:
So let’s start! Tell us a bit about yourself. For example, name, profession, and personal interests.

Hi, I’m Meisa! I’m an industrial engineer who loves the science, technology, and math powering game worlds.

Interesting! What was your latest HackerNoon Top Story about?

It explores the smart set of algorithms generating the 3D world of PEAK.

Do you usually write on similar topics? If not, what do you usually write about?

Yes! I focus on behind game worlds because this is where I find the most interesting mathematical and scientific innovations lie. Creating games is essentially . I believe it’s integral to democratize understanding of how game worlds are constructed, because digital frontiers are the future.

Great! What is your usual writing routine like (if you have one?)

I always start with a (“How was X effect made?” or “Why is X like this?”) to begin my online search. If I stumble on a concept I’ve never heard of, I deep-dive on the topic, using my initial question as the lens to explore it.

While investigating, I tend to slip back into engineering student mode. I dig until I get to the (usually a research paper) and rewrite it into something an elementary schooler would understand to check my own understanding.

When writing, I try to tell a . I start with the question, highlight why it matters, retrace my own steps during the research phase, and end by stating the answer as simply as possible.

Being a writer in tech can be a challenge. It’s not often our main role, but an addition to another one. What is the biggest challenge you have when it comes to writing?

My understanding of the topic changes the more time I spend writing the piece.

When I start on an article with only my technical question as the root, I’m still the target audience because I don’t yet understand the answer. However, when my own understanding reaches a level where I can write about it comfortably, I’m no longer certain if my writing is simple enough for a .

To ensure my writing is still bite-sized and readily consumable, I like sending it to friends or asking my younger siblings (who are still in high school) to read it over!

What is the next thing you hope to achieve in your career?

I’m currently building a game from internet comments, step-by-step, alongside our audience! I’m really looking forward to launching the demo of the game on Steam.

Wow, that’s admirable. Now, something more casual: What is your guilty pleasure of choice?

Cookies… (not the internet kind, but the wonderful carbohydrate kind)

I’m an amateur … which is just a fancy term for being a devout enjoyer of fermentation! I started with sourdough, then moved to kombucha, and currently have my sights set on rice vinegar.

A technical story how modern games perfected horror lighting through a !

I’ve had a great experience writing on HackerNoon. I think you’ve found the point of intersection between human curation and automated technology, deploying both where they’re most effective. For drafting and inspiration, you provide technological tools, but for readership, your own editors chime in to bring a .

Happy to connect! You can send me a note here.
